title: The Two Halves of RAG
concept: The offline indexing pipeline (ingest, chunk, embed, store) and the online query pipeline (retrieve, rerank, generate).

exercises.prompt: How does Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) help solve the problem of LLM hallucinations and protect LLMs from stale data. How does Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) solve this problem?

options.text: By rewriting the LLM's core neural network architecture.
By grounding the LLM's answers in retrieved facts from external data sources.
By forcing the LLM to memorize facts through reinforcement learning.

exercises.explain: RAG connects LLMs to external data sources to ground their answers in facts, which prevents hallucinations and updates the model's knowledge without expensive retraining.

cards.title: Organizing the Exam Prep
cards.body: Imagine trying to write an open-book exam, but the textbook is 10,000 pages long and disorganized. To pass, you need a system to organize the book beforehand, and a quick way to look up the right pages during the test.

cards.title: Offline vs. Online Pipelines
cards.body: RAG is split into two halves: the offline indexing pipeline (which prepares, slices, and stores your documents in a search database) and the online query pipeline (which takes a user's question, searches the database, and uses the results to generate an answer).

cards.title: The Kitchen Analogy
cards.body: Think of RAG like a restaurant. The offline pipeline is the prep chef who chops ingredients and stocks the pantry before opening. The online pipeline is the line chef who takes your order, grabs the right ingredients, and cooks your meal.

cards.title: RAG in Action
cards.body: If you build a customer support bot, your offline pipeline runs overnight to process thousands of PDF manuals. When a user asks a question at noon, the online pipeline instantly retrieves the relevant page and drafts a response.

cards.title: The Real-Time Trap
cards.body: Some developers think RAG processes all documents in real-time when a user asks a question. In reality, doing so would be way too slow and expensive; that is why we pre-process and index documents offline.

cards.title: The RAG Division of Labor
cards.body: Let's compare the duties of the two main halves of a Retrieval-Augmented Generation system.

left.text: Prepares documents by slicing them and saving them in a searchable database.

right.text: Searches the database for a user's query and generates a response.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes the correct order of steps in the offline indexing pipeline?
exercises.explain: The offline pipeline processes raw documents by ingesting them, splitting them into smaller chunks, embedding them into vectors, and storing them in a database.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The online query pipeline is triggered in real-time when a user submits a question.
exercises.explain: The online query pipeline begins when a user submits a query. It retrieves matching documents and feeds them to the LLM to generate the final response.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct pipeline stage type.
exercises.sentence: We use the offline indexing pipeline to prepare and store document chunks before any user asks a question.
exercises.explain: The offline indexing pipeline prepares the documents beforehand so they can be searched quickly when a query arrives.

exercises.prompt: Which of these descriptions of RAG pipelines was generated by a confused AI?

options.text: First, we ingest the PDFs and chunk them. Second, we embed those chunks and save them in our vector database.
During the online phase, the system takes the user's query, finds the best matches in the database, and sends them to the LLM.
The offline pipeline executes backpropagation metrics to align the quantized indexing queries with the live weights.

exercises.explain: Option C is generated by an AI because it hallucinated a chaotic mix of unrelated technical terms ('backpropagation metrics', 'quantized indexing queries') that make no sense in the context of standard RAG pipelines.

exercises.prompt: How do the offline indexing pipeline and the online query pipeline coordinate to serve a user?

options.text: The offline pipeline must run first to prepare the knowledge base before the online pipeline can search it.
The online pipeline runs once a day, while the offline pipeline runs every time a user types a message.
Both pipelines must run simultaneously on the user's phone to generate a single sentence.
The offline pipeline is only used for training the LLM from scratch, while the online pipeline is for fine-tuning.

exercises.explain: The offline pipeline must happen first to prepare and index the documents into a vector database. Only after this is complete can the online pipeline search through those documents to answer a user's query in real-time.
